title: Decoherence Mitigation with Local NOT Gates in Multipartite Systems
authors: Venkat Abhignan, Raghav Sundararaman, Shriram Pragash M, R. Srikanth, Ashutosh Singh
abstract: We study the entanglement dynamics of $n=2,3,4$-qubit Bell- and GHZ-type states under an amplitude-damping channel (ADC). We quantify multipartite entanglement using the genuine multipartite concurrence (GMC) and evaluate its utility through the optimal teleportation fidelity. For $2$-qubit states, we analyze the standard (Bennett) teleportation protocol. For $3$- and $4$-qubit states, we study controlled quantum teleportation (CQT) with one and two \emph{controllers}, respectively. Entanglement sudden death (ESD) denotes the abrupt, finite-time disappearance of entanglement caused by decoherence in contrast to asymptotic decay. To counteract ESD, we apply local NOT ($\hatσ_x$) operations on $m$ of the $n$ qubits ($m \leq n$) and derive analytic formulae, revealing that a single-NOT operation often suffices to alter ESD into asymptotic decay when handling GMC. In contrast, teleportation fidelity can decay more rapidly for single-NOT flipped states, whereas flipping all qubits is more useful for preserving teleportation fidelity in certain regimes, highlighting that the amount of entanglement alone does not guarantee teleportation utility. Remarkably, in the case of GHZ-type states, ADC-evolved mixed biseparable states can be exploited successfully in the CQT protocol. Further, using the GHZ-symmetric parametrization, we map the 2- and 3-qubit ADC-evolved mixed states onto a $(x,y)$ plane, revealing their SLOCC (Stochastic Local Operations and Classical Communication) entanglement classes. We also explicitly check the Bell-CHSH nonlocality hierarchy in the 2-qubit teleportation alongside localizable-entanglement diagnostics for 3-qubit CQT. Our results clarify the distinct roles of global versus localizable bipartite correlations and suggest simple, experimentally accessible unitary controls for preserving useful quantum resources in noisy channels.
summary: This paper studies how local NOT gates can be used to prevent the abrupt disappearance of entanglement (Entanglement Sudden Death) in 2, 3, and 4-qubit systems under amplitude damping. The authors show that while a single NOT gate can often turn sudden death into a slower asymptotic decay for entanglement, the best strategy for preserving teleportation fidelity may differ. The study highlights a decoupling between the amount of entanglement preserved and the actual utility of the state for quantum communication tasks. The results offer practical guidance for using simple $\pi$-pulses to protect quantum information in noisy hardware.
main_problem: Investigating how to mitigate Entanglement Sudden Death (ESD) and preserve the operational utility of multipartite entangled states under amplitude-damping noise.
main_result: Applying local NOT gates can transform entanglement sudden death into asymptotic decay for GMC, though the optimal number of gates depends on the specific noise regime and the metric being preserved (GMC vs. teleportation fidelity).
method: Analytical derivation of entanglement dynamics and teleportation fidelity using GHZ-symmetric parametrization, twirling operations, and the Horodecki criterion for Bell nonlocality.
model_or_system: Multipartite GHZ-type and Bell-type states consisting of 2, 3, and 4 qubits subject to a two-stage Amplitude Damping Channel (ADC).
key_observables: Genuine Multipartite Concurrence (GMC), teleportation fidelity (F), Bell-CHSH parameter (S), and Localizable Entanglement (LE).
important_parameters: Number of qubits (n=2,3,4), number of NOT gates applied (m), damping parameters (p, p'), and initial state population imbalance (alpha).
main_assumptions: Local NOT operations are treated as instantaneous relative to the noise timescale; the ADC is modeled as two sequential, independent processes.
figures_summary: Schematics of the noise/control framework and teleportation protocols; plots of GMC and fidelity dynamics showing ESD vs. ADE; geometric projections of 4-qubit state trajectories in the GHZ-symmetric parameter space; and a summary map of optimal NOT-gate strategies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ESD, defines the noise model and control mechanism, provides analytical derivations for entanglement and fidelity, explores the hierarchy of correlations, classifies states using GHZ-symmetric parameters, and concludes with an optimization strategy for gate application.
why_interesting: It provides simple, experimentally accessible unitary control strategies (local bit-flips) to extend the lifetime of quantum resources in noisy environments, which is highly relevant for superconducting and trapped-ion platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustrating entanglement preservation in multiqubit entangled states under ADC noise using local NOT operations applied to one or more qubits between successive ADC processes. The final NOT gate restores the state to the original population configuration.

Figure 3: CQT using three-qubit GHZ-type state with one controller. (a) schematic diagram [70], and corresponding (b) circuit representation [101]. The dotted lines represent the transfer of classical information, and the solid lines show the transfer of qubits from the entanglement source.

Figure 4: CQT using four-qubit GHZ-type state with two controllers. (a) schematic diagram, and corresponding (b) circuit representation [101]. The dotted lines represent the transfer of classical information, and the solid lines show the transfer of qubits from the entanglement source.

Figure 5: Dynamics of two-, three-, and four-qubit entangled states under initial ADC. Evolution of ρ(0,n)(α, p, 0) for (a) n = 2, (b) n = 3, and (c) n = 4. The states exhibit ESD (GMC death for n ≥3) for |α|2 < 0.5, 0.9, 0.98, respectively, and ADE otherwise.

Figure 10: Dynamics of teleportation fidelity (F) using two-, three-, and four-qubit entangled states with initial ADC (p) and state parameter (|α|2). Only fidelity above the classical limit F = 2/3 is plotted. Teleportation fidelity vs. p, |α|2 for (a) ρ(0,2)(α, p, 0), (b) ρ(0,3)(α, p, 0), and (c) ρ(0,4)(α, p, 0).
